A-004-001-001: The bridge and the full-wave centre-tap both use both halves of the AC cycle, but the bridge applies the whole secondary winding to the load each half-cycle. The centre-tap rectifier only uses half the winding at a time, so for the same total secondary voltage its output is about half. That is why the bridge gives the highest average output voltage for a given secondary.
A-004-001-002: With a capacitor input filter and almost no load, the capacitor charges to the peak voltage (1.414 × RMS) and holds it. On the next half-cycle the diode sees that stored peak plus the opposite peak of the source, so the peak inverse voltage reaches about 2 × 1.414 = 2.8 times the RMS voltage. The distractor 1.4 is only the single peak, which ignores the capacitor's stored charge adding on top.
A-004-001-003: In a full-wave centre-tap supply the non-conducting diode sees the peak across the whole secondary while the other diode conducts, so its reverse voltage equals the full peak-to-peak swing. That is 2 × (1.414 × RMS) = 2.8 times the RMS voltage, and unlike the half-wave case this holds regardless of load. The values 0.636 and 0.707 are average and RMS factors of a sine wave, not the PIV.
A-004-001-004: A bridge rectifier uses four diodes arranged so the full secondary winding feeds the load on both half-cycles, so it does not need the transformer secondary to be split with a centre tap. The centre-tap design must have that tap because each diode only handles one half of the winding. Removing the centre-tap requirement is the bridge's key practical advantage.
A-004-001-005: The bridge uses the entire secondary winding on each half-cycle, while the centre-tap rectifier uses only half the winding at a time, so for the same transformer the bridge delivers about double the output voltage. The catch is that the centre-tap design effectively wastes half its winding, which the bridge avoids. Hence the bridge output is double, not the same or half.
A-004-001-006: A full-wave rectifier produces one output pulse for each half of the AC cycle, so the ripple frequency is twice the line frequency. Canadian household power is 60 Hz, so the ripple is 2 × 60 = 120 Hz. The higher ripple frequency is also easier to filter than the 60 Hz of a half-wave supply.
A-004-001-007: A half-wave rectifier passes only one half of each AC cycle, so it produces just one pulse per cycle and the ripple frequency equals the line frequency. With 60 Hz household power the ripple is therefore 60 Hz. This is the opposite of the full-wave case, where two pulses per cycle give 120 Hz ripple.
A-004-001-008: A full-wave voltage doubler charges capacitors on both halves of the AC waveform and stacks their voltages to roughly double the peak input. Because it works on both half-cycles, its ripple frequency is twice that of a half-wave doubler and it filters more easily. It does not produce four times the voltage; doubling is the whole point.
A-004-001-009: A rectifier diode fails if too much reverse voltage breaks it down or too much forward current overheats it, so the two ratings you must not exceed are peak inverse voltage and average forward current. These directly correspond to the two ways a diode is stressed in a supply. The other choices mix in irrelevant parameters like capacitive reactance that do not describe diode limits.
A-004-001-010: When diodes are stacked in series for high voltage, small differences in their characteristics make them share reverse voltage unequally. A resistor across each diode equalizes the static voltage drops, and a capacitor across each diode bypasses fast transient spikes so no single diode is over-stressed. The aim is voltage equalization and transient protection, not smoothing the output.
A-004-001-011: An unfiltered full-wave rectifier flips both halves of the AC sine wave to the same polarity, producing a train of half-sine humps. Because there are two humps per input cycle, this pulse series occurs at twice the AC input frequency. Without a filter capacitor it is not steady DC; the smoothing only happens once a filter is added.
A-004-002-001: A filter choke must store and pass the DC load current without its iron core saturating, and it must present enough inductance to smooth ripple. So it is rated by inductance (its smoothing ability) and current-handling capacity (before saturation). Breakdown voltage and power loss are not its defining ratings.
A-004-002-002: A choke input filter limits the surge to the capacitor and lets the rectifier conduct more continuously, so the output voltage sags far less between no load and full load. Paired with a full-wave rectifier this gives the best voltage regulation of the choices. A capacitor input filter gives higher voltage but much poorer regulation because output drops sharply as load increases.
A-004-002-003: A capacitor input filter charges to near the peak voltage and holds it, so its main advantage is a higher DC terminal voltage than a choke input filter, which sits closer to the average. The trade-off is poorer regulation and higher peak rectifier currents. So the benefit you gain is the higher output voltage, not better filtering or regulation.
A-004-002-004: A choke input filter conducts current more continuously and limits charging surges, so the output voltage changes little as the load varies. Under a normal load this gives the best-regulated output of the filter types. It does not give the highest voltage; that belongs to the capacitor input filter, which trades away regulation.
A-004-002-005: Power-supply filters are named by which component the rectifier output meets first. If a series choke comes first it is a choke input filter; if a shunt capacitor comes first it is a capacitor input filter. So the two general types are choke input and capacitor input.
A-004-002-006: A bleeder resistor's main job is to safely discharge the filter capacitors after power is removed. Because it always draws a small steady current, it also keeps a minimum load on the supply, which steadies the output voltage and improves voltage regulation. That steadying effect is its useful secondary function.
A-004-002-007: An inductor opposes changes in current, so it presents high reactance to the AC ripple component but very low resistance to steady DC. Placed in series, a choke therefore passes the DC load current freely while impeding the AC ripple. This is exactly the smoothing action wanted from a filter choke.
A-004-002-008: A choke input filter only regulates well when current flows continuously through the choke; below a critical minimum current its behaviour changes and the output voltage jumps up. A bleeder resistor draws a small steady current at all times, guaranteeing that minimum load whenever the supply is on. So the fix is to include a suitable bleeder resistance.
A-004-002-009: The first choke (an inductor) and the first capacitor form an LC pair that can resonate. If their values let them resonate near the ripple frequency, the ripple voltage builds up instead of being smoothed. So these two components, the first choke and first capacitor, must be chosen carefully to keep resonance away from the ripple frequency.
A-004-002-010: If the first choke and first capacitor resonate near the ripple frequency, they form a series resonant circuit whose low impedance lets large currents flow and voltages build up. This can drive excessive rectifier peak current and abnormally high peak inverse voltages on the diodes. Proper component selection keeps this series resonance away from the ripple frequency.
A-004-002-011: Normally the choke input filter holds the capacitor at about nine-tenths of the RMS voltage, but that assumes a load is drawing current. If the bleeder resistor burns out and there is no load, the choke can no longer hold the voltage down and the capacitor charges all the way to the transformer peak voltage. Rating the capacitor for that peak provides the safety margin against this fault condition.
A-004-003-001: A linear regulator works by continuously varying the conduction of its control element (the pass transistor), keeping it partly on so it acts like an automatically adjusted resistor in series with the load. As line voltage or load current changes, its conduction is varied in direct proportion to hold the output steady. Switching the element fully on and off describes a switching regulator instead, which is the tempting wrong answer.
A-004-003-002: A switching regulator rapidly turns its control device fully on and fully off, and it adjusts the duty cycle (the on-versus-off ratio) to suit the line and load conditions. Because the device is either saturated or cut off rather than partly conducting, it dissipates little power and is efficient. Varying conduction in direct proportion describes a linear regulator, not a switcher.
A-004-003-003: A Zener diode held in reverse breakdown maintains a nearly constant voltage across itself over a range of current, making it an ideal stable reference for a linear regulator to compare against. An SCR is a switch, a varactor is a voltage-variable capacitor, and an ordinary junction diode has no sharp regulating point. So the Zener diode is the standard voltage reference.
A-004-003-004: A series regulator places its control element in series with the load and draws only the current the load needs plus a little overhead, so it wastes little of the source. A shunt regulator instead bleeds off excess current continuously, which is less efficient. Where efficient use of the primary power source matters, the series regulator is preferred.
A-004-003-005: A shunt regulator works by diverting more or less current to itself so that the total current drawn from the source stays roughly constant regardless of load. This presents a constant load to the unregulated source, which is exactly the application asked about. A series regulator, by contrast, lets the source current rise and fall with the load.
A-004-003-006: Remote sensing means the regulator's feedback connection to its error amplifier is taken directly at the load rather than at the regulator's own output terminals. This lets the regulator compensate for voltage drop in the wires, holding the voltage constant where it actually matters, at the load. Sensing at the output alone would leave that wiring drop uncorrected.
A-004-003-007: A three-terminal regulator is a complete regulator in one package, containing a voltage reference, an error amplifier, sensing resistors and transistors, and the pass element, with just input, output, and ground pins. It does not supply three different voltages; the name refers to its three terminals. This integration is why such regulators are so simple to use.
A-004-003-008: Besides the range of input voltage it accepts, a three-terminal regulator is specified by the regulated output voltage it produces and the maximum output current it can safely supply. Together these tell you whether it suits your load. Minimum-current figures are not the defining specs, so the correct pair is output voltage and maximum output current.
A-004-003-009: Putting the voltage reference, error amplifier, sensing resistors and transistors, and the pass element all in one package describes the three-terminal regulator. An op-amp, switching, or Zener regulator does not bundle all of those into a single self-contained device with just three pins. This complete integration is the defining feature of the three-terminal type.
A-004-003-010: When very low ripple or tight regulation against current and line swings is needed, a closed-loop amplifier controls the supply, and these electronic regulators fall into two main categories: linear and switching. Linear types vary a pass element's conduction continuously, while switching types chop the power on and off with a controlled duty cycle. So the two categories are linear and switching.
A-004-003-011: A device that packages a reference, a high-gain error amplifier, temperature-compensated sensing resistors and transistors, and a pass element is the familiar three-terminal regulator. The 'three terminal' name comes from its input, output, and common (ground) pins, not from any internal count. The other pin-count names are not standard regulator types.
A-004-004-001: The pass transistor in a series regulator carries the full load current while dropping the difference between its input and output voltage. Power dissipated is voltage across it times current through it, so its dissipation is directly proportional to both the load current and the input/output voltage differential. A large voltage drop combined with high current is what overheats it, which is why heat sinks are needed.
A-004-004-002: Regulation and ripple are best wherever the feedback samples and corrects the voltage, and in a remote-sensed supply the sampling network or error amplifier is connected right at the load. The loop forces that exact point to stay constant and clean. Measuring at the pass transistor output instead leaves any wiring drop beyond it uncorrected.
A-004-004-003: Load resistance here means the equivalent resistance the supply must drive, found from Ohm's law as output voltage divided by the total current it delivers, including the bleeder current. So this is simply the (effective) load resistance of the supply. It is not the rectifier or differential resistance, which describe other things.
A-004-004-004: Static regulation describes how well the output holds up against slow, long-term changes in load resistance, such as gradually adding equipment. It is the steady-state regulation figure. Short-term, rapid load changes are handled by dynamic regulation instead, so the long-term case is static regulation.
A-004-004-005: Dynamic regulation describes how well the supply holds its output during short-term, rapid changes in load, such as the current bursts of an SSB or CW transmitter. It is the supply's response to quick load swings. Slow, long-term load changes are covered by static regulation, so the short-term case is dynamic regulation.
A-004-004-006: Dynamic regulation depends on the supply's ability to deliver sudden bursts of current without sagging, and a larger output capacitor stores more charge to supply those bursts instantly. So increasing the output capacitor improves dynamic regulation. The choke or input capacitor do not directly stiffen the output against fast load changes the way the output capacitor does.
A-004-004-007: An SSB or CW transmitter draws sudden current peaks, and a larger output capacitor stores extra charge to supply those peaks without the voltage dropping. So increasing the output capacitance gives better dynamic regulation. Reversing the electrolytic's polarity would simply destroy it, so that distractor is dangerous as well as wrong.
A-004-004-008: Four diodes wired in a bridge conduct on alternate half-cycles so the load always sees the same polarity, which is the definition of rectification. So in this supply the bridge acts as a rectifier, converting AC into pulsating DC. It does not match impedance or tune anything; its only job there is rectifying.
A-004-004-009: Fuses sit in the AC line before the transformer, where they carry alternating current, and a fuse can equally be placed in the DC output before the load. So the same component, the fuse, can be the one carrying AC at the input and DC near the output. Diodes and chokes do not serve both of those positions in this way.
A-004-004-010: After the electrolytic filter capacitor smooths the rectified DC, that filtered output feeds the voltage regulator, which then holds the final output steady. So the capacitor's output connects to the voltage regulator. It does not feed a pi filter or a matching circuit in this regulated-supply chain.
A-004-004-011: If the supply output ever rises above the regulator's input (for example when input power is removed while a charged capacitor holds the output up), the regulator can be damaged by reverse voltage. A diode across the input and output terminals harmlessly conducts in that situation, protecting the regulator from reverse voltages. Its job is reverse-voltage protection, not RF bypassing or added capacitance.
